title: Right-Triangle Trigonometry
blurb: Three ratios that unlock every right triangle: name the sides, pick the ratio, and solve for any missing side or angle — then aim it at towers, cliffs, and shadows.
buildsOn: The Pythagorean theorem and similar triangles from Geometry.

lessons.title: The Three Ratios
lessons.concreteComparison: Every ramp with the same steepness has the same rise-to-run ratio, whether it's a doormat wedge or a highway overpass — similar triangles guarantee it. Trigonometry bottles that guarantee: three ratios that depend only on the ANGLE, never on the size.
lessons.objective: Name the sides of a right triangle relative to an angle, and compute sine, cosine, and tangent from side lengths.
concept: In a right triangle, sides get names RELATIVE to the acute angle $\theta$ you're working from: the **hypotenuse** is always the side across from the right angle (the longest); the **opposite** side is the one across from $\theta$; the **adjacent** side is the leg touching $\theta$. Move to the other acute angle and opposite/adjacent swap.

The three ratios: $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$ — memorized as **SOH-CAH-TOA**. In the classic 3-4-5 triangle with $\theta$ opposite the side of length 3: $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{5}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{4}{5}$, $\tan\theta = \frac{3}{4}$.

WHY this works: any two right triangles sharing the angle $\theta$ are similar, so their side RATIOS match exactly — the ratios are properties of the angle alone. That's the entire licence for trigonometry: $\sin 35°$ means one specific number, no matter whose triangle you measure it in. And since the ratios use only two sides at a time, the Pythagorean theorem supplies any third side you're missing.
lessons.keyIdea: Sides are named relative to θ (opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse); SOH-CAH-TOA gives three size-proof ratios that belong to the angle itself.

facts.title: SOH-CAH-TOA
facts.explanation: The three ratios, named by the sides they use.

facts.title: Why size never matters
facts.explanation: The ratio belongs to the angle, not the triangle.

facts.title: The third side
facts.explanation: Two sides known ⟹ Pythagoras hands you the third.

workedExamples.statement: In right triangle $ABC$, the right angle is at $C$, $AC = 4$, $BC = 3$, $AB = 5$. Find $\sin A$, $\cos A$, and $\tan A$.
workedExamples.solution: Name the sides from $A$: opposite $= BC = 3$ (across from $A$), adjacent $= AC = 4$ (the leg touching $A$), hypotenuse $= AB = 5$. Then $\sin A = \frac{3}{5}$, $\cos A = \frac{4}{5}$, $\tan A = \frac{3}{4}$. Sanity: $\sin^2 A + \cos^2 A = \frac{9}{25} + \frac{16}{25} = 1$ ✓.

workedExamples.statement: A right triangle has legs $5$ and $12$. Find the hypotenuse, then all three ratios of the angle $\theta$ opposite the side of length $5$.
workedExamples.solution: Pythagoras first: $c = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$. From $\theta$: opposite $= 5$, adjacent $= 12$, hypotenuse $= 13$: $\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{12}{13}$, $\tan\theta = \frac{5}{12}$.

commonMistakes.text: Fixing 'opposite' and 'adjacent' to the picture instead of the angle.
commonMistakes.correction: The names are RELATIVE: the side opposite $A$ is adjacent to $B$. Before writing any ratio, put your finger on the angle you're using and re-derive which leg is across from it and which touches it.

commonMistakes.text: Using the hypotenuse in tan, or a leg-over-leg ratio in sin.
commonMistakes.correction: Only tangent is leg-over-leg (opp/adj); sine and cosine each use the HYPOTENUSE downstairs. If your sine comes out bigger than 1, you've smuggled a leg ratio into it — a sine or cosine can never exceed 1, because no leg outgrows the hypotenuse.

tryIt.statement: A right triangle has legs $8$ and $15$. Find the hypotenuse and $\tan\theta$ for the angle $\theta$ opposite the side of length $8$.
For an acute angle $\theta$, $\cos\theta = \frac{4}{5}$. Find $\sin\theta$ and $\tan\theta$ (build the triangle first).
tryIt.solution: Build it: adjacent 4, hypotenuse 5 ⟹ opposite $= \sqrt{25 - 16} = 3$. So $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{5}$ and $\tan\theta = \frac{3}{4}$. One ratio determines the whole triangle's shape — and therefore the other two ratios.

steps.title: Three sides, three jobs
steps.body: Stand at the acute angle $\theta$ (at $A$ in the figure) and look around the triangle. The side you're staring ACROSS at — $BC$ — is the **opposite**. The leg your angle touches — $AC$ — is the **adjacent**. And the slanted side across from the right angle — $AB$, always the longest — is the **hypotenuse**. Every trig ratio starts with this naming, so make it a reflex: finger on the angle, then name.

steps.title: Finger on the angle
steps.prompt: In the figure, the right angle is at $C$ and $\theta$ sits at $A$. Which side is OPPOSITE $\theta$?
options: It depends on the triangle's size
steps.explanation: Stand at $A$ and look across: you're staring at $BC$. The touching leg $AC$ is the adjacent, and $AB$ (across from the right angle) is the hypotenuse. Size is irrelevant to the naming — and, as you'll see, to the ratios too.

steps.title: SOH-CAH-TOA
steps.body: Now the three stars of the subject: $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$ — chanted for four hundred years as **SOH-CAH-TOA**. Why do these deserve names? Because any two right triangles with the same $\theta$ are SIMILAR, so these ratios come out identical regardless of size. $\sin 35°$ is one fixed number for the whole universe — that's what makes a trig table (or calculator button) possible.

steps.title: The three ratios, live
steps.teach: Drag the angle and watch all three ratios update: steeper angle → opposite grows → sin and tan climb while cos falls. Then grow the triangle without changing the angle — the sides change, the RATIOS don't move. That stubbornness is the whole subject.

steps.title: Read the ratios off the sides
steps.intro: Name the sides from the angle, then write the three fractions.

examples.prompt: Right angle at $C$, $AC = 4$, $BC = 3$, $AB = 5$. Find $\sin A$, $\cos A$, $\tan A$.
steps: From $A$: opposite $= BC = 3$, adjacent $= AC = 4$, hypotenuse $= AB = 5$.

examples.prompt: Legs $5$ and $12$; $\theta$ is opposite the leg of length $5$. Find all three ratios (hypotenuse first).

steps.title: Tangent from the figure
steps.prompt: Right angle at $C$, $AC = 15$, $BC = 8$, $AB = 17$. What is $\tan A$?
steps.explanation: From $A$: opposite $= BC = 8$, adjacent $= AC = 15$. Tangent is the leg-over-leg ratio: $\frac{8}{15}$. Options C and D are $\sin A$ and $\cos A$ — right sides, wrong ratio.

steps.title: Pythagoras completes the triangle
steps.body: A ratio question hands you two sides; the theorem you already own supplies the third whenever you need it. Given $\cos\theta = \frac{4}{5}$: build the triangle — adjacent 4, hypotenuse 5 — and the missing opposite is $\sqrt{5^2 - 4^2} = 3$. Now ALL the ratios are yours: one ratio pins the triangle's shape, and the shape carries everything else.

steps.title: Ratios from figures
steps.intro: Finger on the angle, name the sides, write the fraction.

problems.prompt: Right angle at $C$, $AC = 8$, $BC = 6$, $AB = 10$. What is $\sin A$?
problems.explanation: Opposite $= BC = 6$, hypotenuse $= 10$: $\sin A = \frac{6}{10} = \frac{3}{5}$. (A scaled 3-4-5 — the ratio doesn't notice the scaling.)

problems.prompt: For an acute angle $\theta$, $\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$. What is $\tan\theta$?
In the same triangle ($\sin\theta = \frac{5}{13}$), what is $\sin$ of the OTHER acute angle?
problems.explanation: At the other angle, opposite and adjacent swap: its opposite is 12, so its sine is $\frac{12}{13}$ — which is exactly $\cos\theta$. The two acute angles trade sin and cos; that's the 'co' in cosine.

steps.title: Sine is a translation accident
steps.body: Indian mathematicians called the half-chord 'jya-ardha', shortened to 'jiva'. Arabic scholars wrote it 'jiba' — but Arabic omits vowels, and later Latin translators misread 'jb' as 'jaib', meaning bay or fold, and translated it 'sinus'. Your calculator's SIN button carries a 900-year-old typo.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Name sides FROM the angle: opposite (across), adjacent (touching), hypotenuse (longest, across from the right angle).
SOH-CAH-TOA: sin = opp/hyp, cos = adj/hyp, tan = opp/adj — size-proof by similarity.
One ratio + Pythagoras rebuilds the triangle and yields the other two.

lessons.title: Finding Missing Sides
lessons.concreteComparison: A ladder leans against a wall at a known angle, and you know the ladder's length — the wall height is already determined; you just haven't computed it yet. One angle plus one side LOCKS a right triangle. Trig ratios are how you read the locked-in sides out.
lessons.objective: Solve for unknown sides of right triangles using the appropriate trig ratio, multiplying or dividing as the unknown's position demands.
concept: The three-step recipe: (1) mark the angle and NAME the two sides in play — the one you know and the one you want; (2) pick the ratio that links exactly those two names (opp&hyp → sin, adj&hyp → cos, opp&adj → tan); (3) write the equation and solve.

The unknown's position decides the algebra. Unknown UPSTAIRS: $\sin 30° = \frac{x}{10}$ ⟹ $x = 10\sin 30° = 5$ — multiply. Unknown DOWNSTAIRS: $\sin 30° = \frac{6}{x}$ ⟹ $x = \frac{6}{\sin 30°} = 12$ — divide. Same ratio, opposite moves.

With the special angles $30°, 45°, 60°$ the answers come out EXACT: $\sin 30° = \frac{1}{2}$, $\cos 60° = \frac{1}{2}$, $\tan 45° = 1$, $\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, $\sin 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. Exams love them for precisely this reason — Unit 2 derives where these values come from.
lessons.keyIdea: Name the known and wanted sides, pick the ratio that uses both, then multiply (unknown upstairs) or divide (unknown downstairs).

facts.title: The recipe
facts.explanation: The ratio must link the side you know to the side you want.

facts.title: Position decides
facts.explanation: Unknown upstairs: multiply. Unknown downstairs: divide.

facts.title: Special-angle values
facts.explanation: 30-45-60 answers come out exact — no calculator.

workedExamples.statement: In right triangle $ABC$ (right angle at $C$), $\angle A = 30°$ and the hypotenuse $AB = 10$. Find $BC$, the side opposite $A$.
workedExamples.solution: Known: hypotenuse. Wanted: opposite. The ratio using both: sine. $\sin 30° = \frac{BC}{10}$, so $BC = 10\sin 30° = 10 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 5$. Sanity: opposite the SMALLEST angle sits the shortest side — 5 against hypotenuse 10 ✓.

workedExamples.statement: A ladder leans at $60°$ to the ground, and its base sits $3$ m from the wall. How long is the ladder?
workedExamples.solution: From the $60°$ angle: known adjacent (3 m), wanted hypotenuse — cosine links them. $\cos 60° = \frac{3}{L}$: unknown DOWNSTAIRS, so divide: $L = \frac{3}{\cos 60°} = \frac{3}{1/2} = 6$ m. The steeper the ladder, the smaller the cosine — and the longer the ladder for the same base gap.

commonMistakes.text: Always multiplying: x = 6 sin 30° when the equation was sin 30° = 6/x.
commonMistakes.correction: Look WHERE the unknown sits before touching the algebra. Downstairs unknowns come out by DIVISION: $x = \frac{6}{\sin 30°} = 12$, not $3$. The wrong move shrinks a hypotenuse below a leg — impossible, and a sanity check would catch it.

commonMistakes.text: Picking the ratio by habit (always sine) instead of by the two sides in play.
commonMistakes.correction: The ratio is DICTATED by which sides the problem touches: adjacent-and-hypotenuse has no sine in it — that's cosine's pair. Name the known side and the wanted side out loud first; the ratio then picks itself.

tryIt.statement: Right angle at $C$, $\angle A = 60°$, hypotenuse $AB = 14$. Find $BC$ (opposite $A$) exactly.
From a point $20$ m from a flagpole's base, the pole is seen at $45°$. Find the pole's height — then explain why no calculator was needed.
tryIt.solution: $\tan 45° = \frac{h}{20}$ ⟹ $h = 20\tan 45° = 20 \cdot 1 = 20$ m. At $45°$ the opposite and adjacent legs are EQUAL (the triangle is isosceles), so tangent is exactly 1 — height equals distance, no computation required.

steps.title: One angle + one side = everything
steps.body: Similar triangles taught you that an acute angle fixes a right triangle's SHAPE; one known side then fixes its SIZE. So every other side is already determined — the trig ratios are the reading device. The whole lesson is one recipe: name the two sides in play, pick the ratio that links them, solve.

steps.title: Unknown upstairs: multiply
steps.intro: The wanted side sits on top of the ratio — isolate it with one multiplication.

examples.prompt: Right angle at $C$, $\angle A = 30°$, hypotenuse $AB = 10$. Find $BC$.
Right angle at $C$, $\angle A = 45°$, adjacent $AC = 8$. Find the opposite side $BC$.

steps.title: Unknown downstairs: divide
steps.body: When the side you WANT is the ratio's denominator — usually a wanted hypotenuse — the equation reads $\sin 30° = \frac{6}{x}$. Cross the $x$ over: $x = \frac{6}{\sin 30°} = \frac{6}{1/2} = 12$. Dividing by a number less than 1 makes things BIGGER, which is exactly right: a hypotenuse must beat every leg. If your 'hypotenuse' comes out smaller than the given leg, you multiplied when you should have divided.

steps.title: Which move?
steps.prompt: Right angle at $C$, $\angle A = 30°$, and the OPPOSITE side $BC = 6$. Which computation gives the hypotenuse $AB$?
steps.explanation: $\sin 30° = \frac{6}{AB}$ puts the unknown downstairs: divide. $AB = \frac{6}{1/2} = 12$. Option B's 'hypotenuse' of 3 is SHORTER than the leg 6 — the sanity check screams.

steps.title: The leaning ladder
steps.intro: A classic setup — adjacent known, hypotenuse wanted.

examples.prompt: A ladder leans at $60°$ to the ground; its base is $3$ m from the wall. How long is the ladder?

steps.title: Solve for the side
steps.intro: Name, pick, solve — and sanity-check against the hypotenuse.

problems.prompt: Right angle at $C$, $\angle A = 60°$, hypotenuse $14$. Find $BC$ (opposite $A$), exactly.
problems.explanation: $BC = 14\sin 60° = 14 \cdot \frac{\sqrt3}{2} = 7\sqrt{3} \approx 12.1$ — long, as it should be across from the big $60°$ angle.

problems.prompt: $\angle A = 30°$ and the opposite leg is $4$. Find the ADJACENT leg, exactly.
problems.explanation: $\tan 30° = \frac{4}{adj}$ ⟹ $adj = \frac{4}{\tan 30°} = \frac{4}{1/\sqrt3} = 4\sqrt{3}$. Downstairs unknown, division — and dividing by the small $\tan 30°$ makes the adjacent LONGER than 4, matching a shallow angle.

problems.prompt: Hypotenuse $20$, $\angle A = 45°$. Find the opposite side, exactly.
problems.explanation: $20\sin 45° = 20 \cdot \frac{\sqrt2}{2} = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.1$ — bigger than 10 (the 45° triangle splits its right angle evenly, legs well over half the hypotenuse) and smaller than 20 ✓.

steps.title: Sanity-check against the hypotenuse
steps.body: Every answer gets a two-second audit: legs must be SHORTER than the hypotenuse, and the longer leg sits across from the bigger acute angle. An answer violating either rule isn't 'probably fine' — it's certainly a multiply/divide mix-up. This audit catches the most common trig error in the world at zero cost.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Recipe: name known + wanted sides → the ratio picks itself → solve.
Unknown upstairs: multiply. Unknown downstairs (wanted hypotenuse): divide.
Audit every answer: legs < hypotenuse; bigger angle faces longer leg.

lessons.title: Finding Missing Angles
lessons.concreteComparison: A wheelchair-ramp inspector doesn't measure the ramp's angle with a protractor in the sky — they measure rise and run with a tape, compute the ratio, and ask 'WHICH angle has this tangent?' That reverse question is the inverse trig function.
lessons.objective: Find unknown acute angles from two known sides using inverse trigonometric functions.
concept: Forward trig answers 'given the angle, what's the ratio?' The reverse question — 'given the ratio, what's the angle?' — is answered by the **inverse functions**: $\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$, $\tan^{-1}$ (also written arcsin, arccos, arctan). $\sin\theta = \frac{1}{2}$ ⟹ $\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 30°$.

The recipe mirrors the last lesson: name the TWO KNOWN sides from the mystery angle, build the ratio those names dictate, then apply the matching inverse. Legs 3 and 4 with opposite 3: $\tan\theta = \frac{3}{4}$, so $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) \approx 36.9°$.

The notation trap: $\sin^{-1} x$ is NOT $\frac{1}{\sin x}$ — the $-1$ means 'inverse function' (undo), not 'reciprocal'. And a free check always exists: the two acute angles of a right triangle sum to $90°$, so once you have one, the other is $90° - \theta$ — compute it both ways and they must agree.
lessons.keyIdea: Inverse trig answers 'which angle has this ratio?': build the ratio from two known sides, apply arcsin/arccos/arctan — and the acute angles must sum to 90°.

facts.title: The inverses
facts.explanation: Ratio in, angle out — one acute answer.

facts.title: Not a reciprocal
facts.explanation: The −1 marks the inverse FUNCTION (undo), not division.

facts.title: The free check
facts.explanation: Acute angles of a right triangle are complementary.

workedExamples.statement: Right angle at $C$; the side opposite $A$ is $5$ and the hypotenuse is $10$. Find $\angle A$.
workedExamples.solution: Known: opposite and hypotenuse ⟹ sine. $\sin A = \frac{5}{10} = \frac{1}{2}$, so $A = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 30°$. Check via the complement: $\cos B = \frac{1}{2}$ gives $B = 60°$, and $30° + 60° = 90°$ ✓.

workedExamples.statement: A ramp rises $3$ m over a horizontal run of $4$ m. Find its angle of incline to the nearest degree.
workedExamples.solution: Rise is opposite, run is adjacent ⟹ tangent: $\tan\theta = \frac{3}{4}$. So $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) \approx 36.9° \approx 37°$. Sanity: $\frac{3}{4} < 1 = \tan 45°$, so the angle must land below $45°$ ✓.

commonMistakes.text: Reading sin⁻¹ x as 1/sin x and computing a reciprocal.
commonMistakes.correction: The $-1$ is function-inverse notation: $\sin^{-1}(0.5)$ asks 'which angle has sine $0.5$?' — answer $30°$. The reciprocal $\frac{1}{0.5} = 2$ isn't even a possible sine. When in doubt, write arcsin instead.

commonMistakes.text: Building the ratio upside-down: tan θ = adj/opp.
commonMistakes.correction: The inverse faithfully returns the angle for whatever ratio you feed it — feed it the flipped ratio and you get the OTHER acute angle. Name the sides from the mystery angle first; if your two answers for a triangle's angles don't sum to $90°$, one ratio was inverted.

tryIt.statement: The side adjacent to $\theta$ is $7$ and the hypotenuse is $14$. Find $\theta$.
A $13$ m guy-wire runs from the top of a $12$ m pole to the ground. What angle does the wire make with the GROUND, and how far is its anchor from the pole's base?
tryIt.solution: Anchor distance first: $\sqrt{13^2 - 12^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5$ m. From the ground angle: opposite 12, hypotenuse 13: $\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{13}\right) \approx 67.4°$. Sanity: $\frac{12}{13}$ is close to 1, so the angle should crowd $90°$ — it does.

steps.title: From ratio back to angle
steps.body: So far the angle was given. But surveyors, builders, and pilots usually have it backwards: the SIDES are measurable with a tape, and the ANGLE is the mystery. Enter the inverse functions $\sin^{-1}, \cos^{-1}, \tan^{-1}$: feed in a ratio, get back the acute angle that owns it. $\sin\theta = \frac{1}{2}$? Then $\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 30°$ — the machine that undoes sine.

examples.prompt: A ramp rises $3$ m over a run of $4$ m. Find the incline angle $\theta$ (nearest degree).

steps.title: Which inverse?
steps.prompt: You know the ADJACENT side and the HYPOTENUSE of a mystery angle. Which button finds it?
options: $\cos^{-1}$ — those two sides are cosine's pair
Any of them works on any two sides
steps.explanation: Adjacent-and-hypotenuse is the CAH of the chant: cosine's pair, so $\cos^{-1}$ recovers the angle. The two known sides always dictate the ratio — forward or inverse.

steps.title: The −1 that isn't a power
steps.body: $\sin^{-1} x$ does NOT mean $\frac{1}{\sin x}$. The superscript $-1$ here is function-inverse notation — 'undo the sine' — the same way $f^{-1}$ undoes $f$. To dodge the ambiguity entirely, mathematicians also write **arcsin, arccos, arctan**. (Annoyingly, $\sin^2 x$ DOES mean $(\sin x)^2$. The notation is inconsistent; four centuries of habit won't change it, so we adapt.)

steps.title: Recover the angles
steps.intro: Build the ratio from the two knowns; feed the matching inverse.

problems.explanation: $\cos\theta = \frac{7}{14} = \frac{1}{2}$ ⟹ $\theta = \cos^{-1}(\frac{1}{2}) = 60°$. A small cosine (adjacent only half the hypotenuse) means a BIG angle.
$\tan\theta = \frac{1}{1} = 1$ ⟹ $\theta = \tan^{-1}(1) = 45°$ — equal legs, the perfectly balanced right triangle.

problems.prompt: In a 3-4-5 triangle, the angle opposite the 3-side is $\approx 36.9°$. What is the OTHER acute angle?
problems.explanation: The acute angles sum to $90°$: $90° - 36.9° = 53.1°$. (Direct check: $\tan^{-1}(\frac{4}{3}) \approx 53.1°$ ✓ — both roads agree, as they must.)

steps.title: Your thumb is a rangefinder
steps.body: Sailors estimate angles with body parts at arm's length: a thumb's width spans about $2°$, a fist about $10°$. Combined with inverse trig run backwards — known height, measured angle — you can estimate the distance to a lighthouse of known height with nothing but an outstretched arm. Trigonometry predates the calculator by two millennia and the tape measure by more.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Inverse trig answers the reverse question: ratio in, acute angle out.
Two known sides dictate the ratio, the ratio dictates which inverse.
sin⁻¹ is an undo, not a reciprocal — and A + B = 90° audits every answer for free.

lessons.title: Angles of Elevation & Depression
lessons.concreteComparison: Tilt your chin up to see a rooftop: that tilt above the horizontal is the angle of ELEVATION. Stand on the roof and look down at your friend: the tilt below the horizontal is the angle of DEPRESSION. Every 'how tall / how far' problem in the real world starts with one of these two tilts.
lessons.objective: Translate elevation/depression descriptions into right triangles and solve them for heights and distances.
concept: Both angles are measured FROM THE HORIZONTAL — never from a wall, pole, or slope. **Elevation**: looking up from horizontal. **Depression**: looking down from horizontal. The observer's horizontal line is the reference arm of the angle, every time.

The translation recipe: draw the horizontal at the observer's eye, mark the angle, and complete the right triangle (heights are vertical legs, ground distances horizontal legs). Then it's last lesson's machinery: two named sides + one angle → pick the ratio → solve.

The depression shortcut: the observer's horizontal is PARALLEL to the ground, so the angle of depression (down from the top) EQUALS the angle of elevation seen from the ground point — alternate interior angles from Geometry. In practice: mark the depression angle at the BOTTOM vertex of your triangle and proceed.
lessons.keyIdea: Elevation and depression are tilts from the HORIZONTAL; draw the horizontal, complete the right triangle — and depression at the top equals elevation at the bottom (alternate interior angles).

facts.title: From the horizontal
facts.latex: \text{elevation: up from horizontal;} \;\; \text{depression: down from horizontal}
facts.explanation: The horizontal is the reference arm, never the wall.

facts.title: The swap
facts.explanation: Alternate interior angles across parallel horizontals.

facts.title: The usual ratio
facts.explanation: Height and ground distance are the two legs — tangent country.

workedExamples.statement: From a point $30$ m from a tower's base, the angle of elevation of the top is $60°$. Find the tower's height exactly.
workedExamples.solution: Ground distance 30 (adjacent), height $h$ (opposite): $\tan 60° = \frac{h}{30}$, so $h = 30\tan 60° = 30\sqrt{3} \approx 52$ m. Sanity: at $45°$ the height would equal 30; a steeper $60°$ must give more ✓.

workedExamples.statement: From the top of a $40$ m cliff, a boat is seen at an angle of depression of $30°$. How far is the boat from the cliff's base, exactly?
workedExamples.solution: Swap the angle to the boat: elevation from the boat is also $30°$. From the boat: opposite = cliff = 40, adjacent = distance $d$: $\tan 30° = \frac{40}{d}$, so $d = \frac{40}{\tan 30°} = 40\sqrt{3} \approx 69$ m. Shallow angle, far boat — the geometry agrees.

commonMistakes.text: Measuring the angle of depression from the cliff face or tower instead of the horizontal.
commonMistakes.correction: Depression is DOWN FROM HORIZONTAL — the angle between the flat line of sight and the tilted one. Measured from the vertical wall instead, every answer comes out as the complement (wrong by $90° - \theta$). Draw the horizontal dashed line first, always.

commonMistakes.text: Placing the depression angle inside the triangle at the top vertex without the swap.
commonMistakes.correction: The depression angle sits OUTSIDE the triangle (between horizontal and hypotenuse). Use alternate interior angles to relocate it to the far vertex — where it becomes that point's elevation angle — and only then apply SOH-CAH-TOA.

tryIt.statement: A tree casts a $9$ m shadow when the sun's elevation is $45°$. How tall is the tree?
tryIt.solution: $\tan 45° = \frac{h}{9}$ ⟹ $h = 9 \cdot 1 = 9$ m. At $45°$, every object is exactly as tall as its shadow is long.

tryIt.statement: A drone hovers $50$ m above the ground. Its operator sees it at an angle of elevation of $30°$. How far is the operator from the point directly below the drone, exactly?
tryIt.solution: $\tan 30° = \frac{50}{d}$ ⟹ $d = \frac{50}{\tan 30°} = 50\sqrt{3} \approx 87$ m. The shallow angle stretches the ground distance well past the height.

steps.title: Everything is measured from horizontal
steps.body: Both angles start at the observer's HORIZONTAL line of sight. Chin up toward a rooftop: the tilt above horizontal is the **angle of elevation**. From a cliff, eyes down to a boat: the tilt below horizontal is the **angle of depression**. The single most common error in this topic is measuring from a wall or tower instead — so every solution starts the same way: draw the horizontal, dashed, at the observer's eye.

steps.title: The tower, measured from the ground
steps.intro: Ground distance and height are the two legs — tangent territory.

examples.prompt: From $30$ m away, a tower's top sits at elevation $60°$. Find the height exactly.

steps.title: Depression borrows elevation's angle
steps.body: From a cliff top, the depression angle lives between the horizontal and the downward gaze — OUTSIDE the useful triangle. But the observer's horizontal is parallel to the ground, and the line of sight cuts both: **alternate interior angles** (Geometry, back on stage) make the depression at the top EQUAL the elevation at the bottom. So relocate: mark the same angle down at the boat/car/person, and solve the triangle from there.

steps.title: The cliff and the boat
steps.intro: Relocate the depression angle to the boat, then run the recipe.

examples.prompt: From atop a $40$ m cliff, a boat sits at depression $30°$. How far is the boat from the cliff's base, exactly?
steps: Swap: depression $30°$ at the top = elevation $30°$ at the boat (alternate interior).

steps.title: Spot the angle
steps.prompt: A pilot at altitude looks DOWN at a runway at an angle of depression of $15°$. What is the angle of elevation of the plane, seen from the runway?
options: $15°$ — alternate interior angles across parallel horizontals
steps.explanation: The pilot's horizontal and the ground are parallel; the line of sight crosses both, making the two angles alternate interior — equal, at any altitude. This swap is the standard first move of every depression problem.

steps.title: Heights and distances
steps.intro: Draw the horizontal, complete the triangle, pick the ratio.

problems.prompt: A tree casts a $9$ m shadow under a $45°$ sun. How tall is the tree?
problems.explanation: $h = 9\tan 45° = 9$. At $45°$, height = shadow — the sun's rays run the diagonal of a square.

problems.prompt: An observer $60$ m from a building sees its top at elevation $30°$. Find the building's height, exactly.
problems.explanation: $h = 60\tan 30° = 60 \cdot \frac{\sqrt3}{3} = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.6$ m. (Option B multiplied by $\tan 60°$ — the classic wrong-angle slip.)

problems.prompt: From a $25$ m lighthouse, a ship is seen at depression $45°$. How far is the ship from the lighthouse's base?
problems.explanation: Swap the $45°$ to the ship: $\tan 45° = \frac{25}{d}$ ⟹ $d = 25$ m. At $45°$ depression, distance = height — the square's diagonal again.

steps.title: Measuring Everest from 240 km away
steps.body: In 1852, surveyors of the Great Trigonometrical Survey of India computed Peak XV's height using elevation angles sighted from stations up to 240 km away — pure triangle trigonometry, no climbing. Their answer: 29,002 feet. The modern GPS value: 29,032 feet. An error of 0.1%, a century before satellites — with the mathematics of this lesson.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Elevation = up from horizontal; depression = down from horizontal — draw the dashed horizontal first.
Depression at the top relocates to elevation at the bottom (alternate interior angles).
Height and ground distance are legs: tangent solves most of these in one line.

practice.statement: In right triangle $ABC$ (right angle at $C$), $AC = 12$, $BC = 5$, $AB = 13$. Write $\sin A$, $\cos A$, and $\tan A$.
practice.solution: From $A$: opposite $= BC = 5$, adjacent $= AC = 12$, hypotenuse $= 13$. $\sin A = \frac{5}{13}$, $\cos A = \frac{12}{13}$, $\tan A = \frac{5}{12}$.

practice.statement: For an acute angle $\theta$, $\tan\theta = \frac{8}{15}$. Find $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$.
practice.solution: Build the triangle: opposite 8, adjacent 15, hypotenuse $\sqrt{64 + 225} = 17$. $\sin\theta = \frac{8}{17}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{15}{17}$.

practice.statement: Right angle at $C$, $\angle A = 30°$, hypotenuse $AB = 26$. Find both legs exactly.
A kite string is $50$ m long and makes a $60°$ angle with the ground. How high is the kite, exactly?
practice.solution: Height is opposite, string is hypotenuse: $h = 50\sin 60° = 25\sqrt{3} \approx 43$ m.

practice.statement: The side opposite $\theta$ is $9$ and the adjacent side is $9\sqrt{3}$. Find $\theta$ exactly.
A $10$ m ladder reaches $8$ m up a wall. What angle does it make with the GROUND (nearest degree), and how far is its base from the wall?
From $45$ m away, the top of a monument sits at elevation $45°$; from the same spot its flag-tip sits at elevation $60°$. Find the monument's height and the flag-tip's height, exactly.
practice.solution: Monument: $45\tan 45° = 45$ m. Flag-tip: $45\tan 60° = 45\sqrt{3} \approx 78$ m. (The flag itself is the difference, $45\sqrt3 - 45 \approx 33$ m.)

practice.statement: From the top of a $60$ m tower, a car is seen at an angle of depression of $60°$. How far is the car from the tower's base, exactly?
practice.solution: Swap to the car: elevation $60°$ there. $\tan 60° = \frac{60}{d}$ ⟹ $d = \frac{60}{\sqrt3} = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 35$ m. Steep angle, close car.

testYourself.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $25$ and one leg $7$. Find the other leg and all three ratios of the angle $\theta$ opposite the 7-leg.
For an acute angle $\theta$, $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{5}$. Compute $\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ and confirm it equals $\tan\theta$.
testYourself.solution: Triangle: 3-4-5, so $\cos\theta = \frac{4}{5}$. $\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \frac{3/5}{4/5} = \frac{3}{4} = \tan\theta$ ✓ — sine over cosine IS tangent, always (both are opp and adj, each divided by hyp).

testYourself.statement: Right angle at $C$, $\angle B = 45°$, and $BC = 6$. Find $AC$ and $AB$ exactly.
testYourself.solution: From $B$: $AC$ is opposite, $BC = 6$ adjacent: $AC = 6\tan 45° = 6$. Hypotenuse: $AB = \frac{6}{\cos 45°} = 6\sqrt{2}$. The 45° triangle: equal legs, hypotenuse $\sqrt2$ times a leg.

testYourself.statement: A zipline runs from a $30$ m platform to the ground, meeting it at $30°$. How long is the zipline?
A ramp must rise $2$ m and may have at most a $30°$ incline. What is the SHORTEST allowed horizontal run, exactly — and does a $3$ m run comply?
testYourself.solution: At exactly $30°$: $\tan 30° = \frac{2}{run}$ ⟹ run $= \frac{2}{\tan 30°} = 2\sqrt{3} \approx 3.46$ m — the shortest legal run. A 3 m run is SHORTER, making the ramp steeper than $30°$: not compliant.

testYourself.statement: Two buildings stand $24$ m apart. From the roof of the shorter ($18$ m), the top of the taller is at elevation $30°$. Find the taller building's height, exactly.
testYourself.solution: From the shorter roof, horizontal 24 m to the taller building: extra height $= 24\tan 30° = 8\sqrt{3}$. Total: $18 + 8\sqrt{3} \approx 31.9$ m.
